title_en: Al-Majmu's Completion by as-Subki and al-Mutii
title_ar: إكمال المجموع على يد السبكي والمطيعي
content_en:
When an-Nawawi died in 676 AH without completing al-Majmu', he left behind a masterpiece that covered the chapters of worship with unparalleled depth but fell silent precisely at the point where Islamic law opens into the vast domain of commerce, contracts, family, inheritance, and the rest of mu'amalat. For centuries, Shafi'i scholars felt the weight of this incompleteness, consulting the finished volumes constantly while lacking a comparably authoritative source for the chapters that an-Nawawi never reached.

The first major attempt to complete al-Majmu' came from Taqi ad-Din Ali ibn Abd al-Kafi as-Subki (d. 756 AH), one of the foremost Shafi'i scholars of the eighth Islamic century and the father of the celebrated Taj ad-Din as-Subki. Taqi ad-Din was a judge and scholar of immense learning who had mastered hadith, fiqh, and usul, and who understood the spirit and methodology of an-Nawawi's work well enough to attempt a continuation in the same style. His contribution added several volumes picking up from where an-Nawawi stopped, covering aspects of the bai' chapter and some subsequent material.

However, as-Subki's completion also remained unfinished. The project of completing al-Majmu' turned out to be a task that required extraordinary sustained effort, and the demands of scholarly and judicial life in the mamluk period left even this capable scholar unable to bring the work to its natural conclusion. The result was that al-Majmu' existed for centuries in a partially completed state — with an-Nawawi's portion, as-Subki's addition, and then silence.

The completion that brought al-Majmu' to its full twenty-three-volume form in the standard modern edition came from Shaykh Muhammad Najib al-Mutii (d. 1935 CE), an Egyptian Shafi'i scholar who dedicated the later part of his scholarly life to finishing what an-Nawawi and as-Subki had begun. Al-Mutii was a product of al-Azhar who had mastered the classical Shafi'i tradition, and his completion was based on the same comparative fiqh methodology: presenting the Shafi'i positions, engaging with the other madhhabs, and citing the relevant hadiths and scholarly opinions. His work on the remaining volumes — covering bai', nikah, divorce, inheritance, and other major chapters — brought the project to a genuine completion.

The question of how to read and use the different sections of al-Majmu' is important for scholars. An-Nawawi's original portion (roughly the first nine volumes) carries the highest authority, and a position stated there by an-Nawawi is virtually definitive in the Shafi'i school. The sections completed by as-Subki carry significant but slightly lesser weight, as a continuation rather than an original composition. Al-Mutii's completion, valuable and competent as it is, represents a modern scholar working within the tradition rather than a medieval master, and is typically cited with that caveat.

The completed al-Majmu' in its modern printed form, most commonly in the twenty-three-volume edition published in Egypt, is the standard reference library for Shafi'i scholarship worldwide. It sits on the shelves of every serious Shafi'i scholar and student, and the fact that it was brought to completion — even across three scholars and seven centuries — reflects the commitment of the Shafi'i tradition to preserving and transmitting its jurisprudential heritage. Al-Majmu' in its completed form remains the definitive encyclopedia of Shafi'i fiqh, a monument to the tradition's intellectual ambition and the scholars who served it.
